questions.stem: A regular parametrized surface is a map $x : U \to \mathbb{R}^3$ on an open set $U \subset \mathbb{R}^2$ that is differentiable and satisfies, at every point:
questions.explanation: Regularity requires that the partial derivatives $x_u$ and $x_v$ be linearly independent, equivalently $x_u \times x_v \ne 0$. This makes the differential injective and guarantees a well-defined tangent plane at each point.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: The condition $x_u \times x_v \ne 0$ is equivalent to saying that:
questions.explanation: Two vectors have nonzero cross product exactly when they are linearly independent (neither is a scalar multiple of the other). They need not be orthogonal or of unit length; in fact parallel vectors give a zero cross product.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: The tangent plane $T_p S$ to a regular surface at a point $p = x(u_0, v_0)$ is:
options: the line through $p$ in the direction $x_u$.
the span of $x_u$ and $x_v$ at that point.
the plane perpendicular to both $x_u$ and $x_v$.
questions.explanation: The tangent plane is the two-dimensional subspace spanned by the partial derivatives $x_u$ and $x_v$. Every tangent vector to a curve on the surface through $p$ is a linear combination of these two.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: The unit normal vector $N$ to a regular surface is given by:
questions.explanation: The cross product $x_u \times x_v$ is perpendicular to the tangent plane; normalizing it gives the unit normal $N = \dfrac{x_u \times x_v}{|x_u \times x_v|}$. The sum $x_u + x_v$ lies in the tangent plane, not normal to it.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: For the graph of a function $z = f(x, y)$, parametrized by $x(u, v) = (u, v, f(u, v))$, the partial derivative $x_u$ is:
questions.explanation: Differentiating $x(u,v) = (u, v, f(u,v))$ with respect to $u$ gives $x_u = (1, 0, f_u)$. Likewise $x_v = (0, 1, f_v)$. Every graph of a differentiable function is automatically a regular surface.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: For the graph $x(u, v) = (u, v, f(u, v))$, the cross product $x_u \times x_v$ equals:
questions.explanation: With $x_u = (1,0,f_u)$ and $x_v = (0,1,f_v)$, the cross product is $(0\cdot f_v - f_u\cdot 1,\ f_u\cdot 0 - 1\cdot f_v,\ 1\cdot 1 - 0) = (-f_u, -f_v, 1)$. Its third component $1$ shows a graph is always regular.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: A standard parametrization of the cylinder of radius $a$ about the $z$-axis is:
options: $x(u, v) = (a\cos u\cos v, a\cos u\sin v, a\sin u)$.
questions.explanation: The right circular cylinder $x^2 + y^2 = a^2$ is parametrized by $(a\cos u, a\sin u, v)$: the angle $u$ runs around the circle and $v$ moves along the axis. The last option is a parametrization of the sphere.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: For the cylinder $x(u, v) = (a\cos u, a\sin u, v)$, the unit normal at a point is:
questions.explanation: Here $x_u = (-a\sin u, a\cos u, 0)$ and $x_v = (0,0,1)$, so $x_u \times x_v = (a\cos u, a\sin u, 0)$ with magnitude $a$. Normalizing gives $N = (\cos u, \sin u, 0)$, pointing radially outward, perpendicular to the axis.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: A surface of revolution obtained by rotating the profile curve $(f(v), g(v))$ (with $f > 0$) about the $z$-axis is parametrized by:
questions.explanation: Rotating the point $(f(v), 0, g(v))$ through angle $u$ about the $z$-axis sends it to $(f(v)\cos u, f(v)\sin u, g(v))$. Here $f(v)$ is the distance to the axis and $g(v)$ is the height; $u$ is the angle of rotation.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: On a surface of revolution, the circles traced by fixing $v$ and varying $u$ are called:
questions.explanation: Curves of constant $v$ (fixed profile point, rotating) are the parallels (circles of latitude). Curves of constant $u$ (a fixed copy of the profile curve) are the meridians. This matches the geography of a globe.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: The sphere of radius $a$ can be parametrized (away from the poles) by:
options: $(a\cos u\cos v,\ a\cos u\sin v,\ a\sin u)$.
$(u, v, \sqrt{a^2 - u^2 - v^2})$ on all of $\mathbb{R}^2$.
questions.explanation: With latitude $u \in (-\pi/2, \pi/2)$ and longitude $v$, the map $(a\cos u\cos v, a\cos u\sin v, a\sin u)$ covers the sphere except the poles, where $x_u \times x_v = 0$. The first option is a cylinder; the hemisphere graph is only regular for $u^2 + v^2 < a^2$.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: For the sphere parametrization $x(u, v) = (a\cos u\cos v, a\cos u\sin v, a\sin u)$, the unit normal points:
options: radially, parallel to the position vector $x$.
questions.explanation: On a sphere centred at the origin, the outward normal at any point is the radial direction, i.e. parallel to the position vector $x(u,v)$. Indeed $x_u \times x_v$ comes out proportional to $x$, so $N = \pm x/|x|$.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: Compute $x_u \times x_v$ for the graph $z = u^2 + v^2$ at the point $(u, v) = (1, 1)$.
questions.explanation: Here $f = u^2 + v^2$, so $f_u = 2u$, $f_v = 2v$, and $x_u \times x_v = (-f_u, -f_v, 1) = (-2u, -2v, 1)$. At $(1,1)$ this is $(-2, -2, 1)$, with magnitude $\sqrt{4 + 4 + 1} = 3$.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: Which of the following is a tangent vector to the surface at $p = x(u_0, v_0)$?
questions.explanation: Tangent vectors are exactly the linear combinations of $x_u$ and $x_v$, so $3x_u - 2x_v$ is tangent. The normal $N$ and the cross product $x_u \times x_v$ are perpendicular to the tangent plane, hence not tangent (unless zero).
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: The plane through a point $p_0$ with linearly independent direction vectors $w_1, w_2$ can be parametrized as a regular surface by:
questions.explanation: The affine map $x(u,v) = p_0 + u w_1 + v w_2$ has $x_u = w_1$, $x_v = w_2$, which are linearly independent, so $x_u \times x_v \ne 0$ and the plane is a regular surface. Its tangent plane is the plane itself at every point.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: Why does the latitude-longitude parametrization of the sphere fail to be regular at the poles?
options: Because the map is not continuous there.
Because $x_v = 0$ at the poles, so $x_u \times x_v = 0$.
Because the sphere is not a surface there.
questions.explanation: At a pole $u = \pm\pi/2$, we have $\cos u = 0$, and $x_v = (-a\cos u\sin v, a\cos u\cos v, 0) = 0$. With $x_v = 0$ the cross product vanishes, so the parametrization (not the sphere) loses regularity there.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: A subset $S \subset \mathbb{R}^3$ is a regular surface if each point has a neighborhood admitting a parametrization that is a homeomorphism onto its image, is differentiable, and:
questions.explanation: The third defining condition is that the differential $dx_q$ is injective at every $q$, equivalently $x_u \times x_v \ne 0$. Together with being a differentiable homeomorphism onto its image, this is do Carmo's definition of a regular surface.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: For the cylinder $x(u, v) = (a\cos u, a\sin u, v)$, the magnitude $|x_u \times x_v|$ equals:
questions.explanation: With $x_u = (-a\sin u, a\cos u, 0)$ and $x_v = (0,0,1)$, the cross product is $(a\cos u, a\sin u, 0)$, whose magnitude is $\sqrt{a^2\cos^2 u + a^2\sin^2 u} = a$. This constant value will reappear as the area element.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: A change of parameters between two overlapping parametrizations of a regular surface is always:
options: a differentiable map with differentiable inverse (a diffeomorphism).
questions.explanation: A central theorem states that the transition map between two regular parametrizations is a diffeomorphism of the corresponding open sets in $\mathbb{R}^2$. This is what makes notions like differentiability of functions on a surface well defined, independent of the chart.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: Reversing the order of the parameters (swapping $u$ and $v$) changes the unit normal $N = \dfrac{x_u \times x_v}{|x_u \times x_v|}$ how?
questions.explanation: Since $x_v \times x_u = -(x_u \times x_v)$, swapping the parameters reverses the cross product and hence flips the unit normal to $-N$. The normal direction is thus tied to the orientation (order) of the parametrization.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: Consider the cylinder $x(u, v) = (3\cos u, 3\sin u, v)$ of radius $3$.
questions.solution: Differentiating, $x_u = (-3\sin u, 3\cos u, 0)$ and $x_v = (0, 0, 1)$. Their cross product has magnitude $|x_u \times x_v| =$ {{1}}. The unit normal points in the {{2}} direction. Since the cross product is never zero, the parametrization is everywhere {{3}}.
questions.explanation: $x_u \times x_v = (3\cos u, 3\sin u, 0)$ has magnitude $3$. Normalizing gives $N = (\cos u, \sin u, 0)$, radial and perpendicular to the axis. The magnitude $3 \ne 0$ everywhere, so the surface is regular.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: For the graph $z = f(u, v) = u^2 + v^2$ (a paraboloid), compute the normal at the point $(u, v) = (1, 2)$.
questions.solution: The partials are $f_u = 2u$ and $f_v = 2v$, so $x_u \times x_v = (-2u, -2v, 1)$. At $(1, 2)$ this vector is $(-2,$ {{1}}$, 1)$, and its magnitude is {{2}} (two decimals). A graph is regular everywhere because the third component of this cross product is always {{3}}.
questions.explanation: At $(1,2)$: $-2u = -2$, $-2v = -4$, so the cross product is $(-2, -4, 1)$. Its magnitude is $\sqrt{4 + 16 + 1} = \sqrt{21} \approx 4.58$. The third component is always $1$, so the cross product never vanishes.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: Describe the standard sphere parametrization $x(u, v) = (a\cos u\cos v, a\cos u\sin v, a\sin u)$ of radius $a$.
questions.solution: Fixing $u$ and varying $v$ traces a circle of latitude, called a {{1}}. Fixing $v$ and varying $u$ traces a {{2}}. The parametrization fails to be regular at the {{3}}, where $\cos u = 0$.
questions.explanation: Constant latitude $u$ gives circles of latitude (parallels); constant longitude $v$ gives meridians. At the poles $u = \pm\pi/2$, $\cos u = 0$ makes $x_v = 0$, so $x_u \times x_v = 0$ and regularity fails.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: A plane is parametrized by $x(u, v) = (1, 0, 2) + u(1, 1, 0) + v(0, 1, 1)$.
questions.solution: Here $x_u = (1, 1, 0)$ and $x_v = (0, 1, 1)$, which are linearly independent, so the map is a {{1}} surface. Its tangent plane at every point is {{2}}. The cross product $x_u \times x_v = (1, -1, 1)$ has magnitude {{3}} (two decimals).

options: a different plane at each point

questions.explanation: $x_u, x_v$ are independent so the plane is regular. A plane is its own tangent plane at every point. $x_u \times x_v = (1\cdot 1 - 0\cdot 1,\ 0\cdot 0 - 1\cdot 1,\ 1\cdot 1 - 1\cdot 0) = (1, -1, 1)$ with magnitude $\sqrt{3} \approx 1.73$.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: Recall how the unit normal of a parametrized surface depends on the parametrization.
questions.solution: The unit normal is $N = (x_u \times x_v)/|x_u \times x_v|$. If we swap the roles of $u$ and $v$, the cross product changes sign, so $N$ is replaced by {{1}}. The normal is always {{2}} to the tangent plane, and it is well defined precisely because regularity makes $|x_u \times x_v|$ {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Swapping parameters gives $x_v \times x_u = -(x_u \times x_v)$, so $N \to -N$. The cross product is perpendicular to both $x_u$ and $x_v$, hence to the whole tangent plane. Dividing requires $|x_u \times x_v| \ne 0$, which is the regularity condition.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: Build a surface of revolution by rotating the profile curve $(f(v), g(v)) = (2 + \cos v,\ \sin v)$ about the $z$-axis (a torus profile, with $f > 0$).
questions.solution: The parametrization is $x(u, v) = (f(v)\cos u,\ f(v)\sin u,\ g(v))$, where $u$ is the {{1}} of rotation. Since $f(v) = 2 + \cos v$ stays positive (its minimum value is {{2}}), the distance to the axis never reaches zero, so the parallels are genuine circles. The resulting surface is a {{3}}.
questions.explanation: In $(f(v)\cos u, f(v)\sin u, g(v))$ the parameter $u$ is the rotation angle. The profile distance $f(v) = 2 + \cos v$ has minimum $2 - 1 = 1 > 0$, so the circle of rotation never collapses. Rotating an offset circle yields a torus.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces
